title: Floquet-induced suppression of thermalization in a quasiperiodic Ising chain
authors: Biswajit Paul, Nilanjan Roy, Tapan Mishra
abstract: Many-body localized (MBL) systems are known to thermalize in periodically driven systems. In this work, we demonstrate that under proper driving protocol, this thermalization this thermalization can be resisted such that the MBL phase turns into a non-ergodic extended phase, known as the many-body critical (MBC) phase. Considering a kicked quasiperiodic Ising chain, we show that while at high-frequency driving the ergodic, MBL, and the MBC phases coexist, at moderate driving frequencies the MBL phase is completely suppressed and the MBC phase proliferates in the parameter space. Using quasienergy statistics, Floquet eigenstates, autocorrelation dynamics, and entanglement growth, we characterize the emergent phases and identify non-monotonic signatures revealing richness of the nonergodic phases. Our results establish Floquet driving as a powerful route to stabilizing nonergodic extended many-body phases beyond the conventional Floquet-MBL paradigm.
summary: This paper shows that periodic driving in a quasiperiodic Ising chain can suppress many-body localization to create a stable, non-ergodic extended phase called the Many-Body Critical phase. It identifies how driving frequency controls the transition between ergodic, MBL, and MBC regimes. This discovery offers a new way to engineer non-ergodic dynamics in driven quantum systems.
main_problem: Investigating the stability of many-body localization (MBL) under periodic driving and exploring whether a non-ergodic extended phase, known as the Many-Body Critical (MBC) phase, can be stabilized or expanded.
main_result: The study demonstrates that Floquet driving can suppress the MBL phase and instead promote a robust, broad Many-Body Critical (MBC) phase, particularly at moderate driving frequencies.
method: Numerical exact diagonalization on finite system sizes, use of the Magnus expansion to analyze driving regimes, and scaling analysis of level statistics and entanglement growth.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, periodically driven (kitted) quasiperiodic Ising spin-1/2 chain with alternating transverse and longitudinal fields.
key_observables: Level spacing ratio (LSR), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), autocorrelation function, and half-system entanglement entropy.
important_parameters: Driving period (T), quasiperiodicity strengths (delta and lambda), transverse field (mu), and interaction strength (J).
main_assumptions: The use of the Magnus expansion assumes high-frequency driving can be related to a time-averaged static Hamiltonian; finite system size limitations (L=9 to 12).
figures_summary: The figures illustrate phase diagrams showing the coexistence or suppression of Ergodic, MBL, and MBC phases, and plots of level spacing ratios and entanglement entropy growth across different driving frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Floquet-induced thermalization, describes the quasiperiodic Ising model, details the numerical and analytical methods (Magnus expansion, scaling), presents the results for high and moderate frequency regimes, and discusses the physical implications and potential applications.
why_interesting: It provides a counter-intuitive mechanism where periodic driving, which usually drives systems toward ergodicity, can be used to stabilize a specific type of non-ergodic extended phase (MBC), offering new routes for controlling transport and information in driven many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Phase diagram: (a) At driving period T = 0.02π (high frequency), the ergodic, MBC, and MBL phases are sep- arated by solid lines and markers. A Widom-like line emerges from the triple point inside the MBL phase as denoted by the dashed line. (b) At T = 1.5π (moderate driving frequency), we observe mainly two phases i.e. ergodic and MBC phases which are separated by the solid black line. Deep within the MBC phase, we observe a dynamical crossover line, indicated by a dotted line in the figure.

FIG. 2. In figures (a) and (b) the averaged-LSR (⟨r⟩) is plot- ted as a function of δ for increasing system sizes L, for λ = 0.3 and λ = 0.8, respectively, for fixed T = 0.02π. Figures (c) and (d) show similar plots, but now for T = 1.5π for increasing system sizes while keeping the other parameter values same as in Fig. (a) and (b). Inset shows scaling and collapse of data as ⟨r⟩∼(δ −δc)L1/ν.

FIG. 3. Figures (a) and (b) show energy- resolved ⟨r⟩for δ = 0.4 and δ = 0.6, respectively for system sizes L = 11, 12, while keeping J = 0.2, µ = 0.2, and λ = 0.8 fixed. Figures (c) and (d) show the probability distribution (P(r)) for λ = 0.4 and λ = 0, 6, respectively, for increasing values of δ. We keep J = 0.2, µ = 0.2, and L = 12 fixed for these two figures.

Fig. 2(d), respectively. It is important to note that the MBL phase of the high-frequency regime becomes an MBC phase in the limit of low-frequency driving, indi- cating proliferation of the MBC phase with decreasing driving frequency. Interestingly, lowering the driving fre- quency, even further, shows the shifting of the crossover line towards a lower value of δ tracing (µ + δ)T ≈π. However, the ergodic-MBC transition still happens at δc = µ, irrespective of λ. This robust MBC phase ap- pears for δ ≥µ due to the appearance of near-zero num- bers in the distribution of gj (i.e. the strength of spin- flipping terms), which maximizes at δ = µ and contin- ues to persist for δ > µ [21]. FIG. 4. Figures (a) and (b) show the autocorrelation C as a function of the Floquet-step n for driving period T = 0.02π and T = 1.5π, respectively. Figures (c) and (d) illustrate the half-system EE (SL/2) as a function of Floquet-step n for the same values of T as in panels (a) and (b), respectively. We keep the other parameter fixed at µ = 0.2, J = 0.2 and λ = 0.7. For all plots L = 16. The dashed lines in figures (b) and (d) represent the fitted curve. The smooth solid lines for the fluctuating data in (b) are obtained using the spline method (a global interpolation method) implemented with the Python-based SciPy [26, 27] library. The insets show various exponents as function of δ.
